It stinks, right? Don't feel alone. It's just one of those things that happens to everyone. I asked you this question because it highlights a universal constant. That is, no one likes to be rejected. It's an emotional event. You begin to feel sad, discouraged, or angry and wonder why that person would reject your offer. What did you do wrong? The buildup of emotions can also take place during a negotiation. And, as you already know, people are very emotionally driven. It doesn't help you to upset or disappoint the other party regularly. So, if rejecting an offer can cause emotions to flare, how do you reject an offer that isn't desirable for you while avoiding the negative accumulation of emotions? I'll give you a hint. Calibrated how questions. And if you've been paying attention so far, then you also know that they are a gentle and subtle way to reject the offer without rejecting the person and without being abrasive. This is also known as letting know out a little at a time. It's one of those things where implying no instead of bluntly saying it causes the other side to think through faster and become more collaborative. Therefore, it has a more effective impact. Why and how? Simply because you're being indirect about your rejection, your counterpart won't shut down or feel like their pride is being hurt. In fact, these responses sound so much like counterbids that they will often go ahead and soften their position or keep bidding against themselves to try and make the deal. As it turns out, you can get away with expressing no three times before actually having to say the word. As a great rule of thumb, you'd better plan on saying no no fewer times than twice. There are a significant number of negotiators out there that are hardwired to not let up on you when you've given only one no. They will keep pushing and pushing you until you say it at least twice. They figure if you haven't given them a no at least twice, there's more concessions for them to squeeze out of you. Those same tough bargainers are going to say no to you at least twice to see if they can get you to bid against yourself as well. So you need this progression as an effective means of protecting yourself. The first no in your series is our good old reliable, how am I supposed to do that? The key here is to deliver it so it sounds like you're petitioning them for help. It invites the other party to participate in your predicament and solve with a better offer. Chances are you will use this two or three times before you even move on to the next phase. The second no is going to be some form of, your offer is very generous. I'm sorry, that just doesn't work for me. The use of the word generous encourages your counterpart to live up to the word while letting you avoid making an actual counteroffer. By apologizing, you're not only building up the relationship but also softening the impact the no has on them. Despite what some pundits say, apologizing isn't necessarily a sign of weakness. The phrase is context driven. Use strategically, which is before you've delivered the bad news. It can create the dynamic of forced empathy and can be quite powerful. Next, you can use something to the effect of, I'm sorry, but I'm afraid I just can't do that. This is a subtle change yet more direct. The phrase I just can't do that works on two levels. Since you're indicating that you're unable to perform a specific action, your counterpart will feel empathetic towards your situation, which has a strong influence on their behavior. The second advantage of I just can't do that is it removes the lever of external criteria from being used against you. Many aggressive counterparts are going to want to find an external criteria which may or may not be comparable to take you hostage with. This short circuits that affect and takes it off the table. The final way you can reject their offer is the more concise and direct, I'm sorry, no. If you deliver this gently, then it will barely register as being negative. And if you do have to go one step further, you can deliver a final, no. As your fifth and last resort, this is the most direct form and should not come out as no. When using this response, make sure to deliver it with a downward inflection and a tone of respect. Remember, you're not trying to make the other side resentful, but you also don't want to agree to something that isn't suitable for you or the company you represent. No deal is better than a bad deal.
